patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 34-year-old elementary school teacher with a clear, cooperative style. Start with: “My legs have been getting stiffer and I keep tripping when I walk.” Early in the visit, volunteer: legs feel tight/stiff (worse when first starting to walk), more tripping/awkward gait noticed by others, stairs harder over the last couple years, and you tire faster on longer walks. If asked, add: feet sometimes drag/can’t lift well, nighttime leg jerks/twitches waking you, calf cramps after walking, worse balance on uneven ground, stiffness worse with stress or cold. Do not mention urinary urgency, constipation, uneven shoe wear/toeing-in, or holding onto furniture unless specifically asked about bladder/bowel symptoms, gait changes, falls, or home safety. If directly asked about leakage, be hesitant and embarrassed; admit a couple small leaks before reaching the toilet. Mention mild low back soreness and wonder about a pinched nerve; also say you Googled and worry about multiple sclerosis. Express cost/insurance worries about genetic testing, limited PT coverage, and fear a genetic diagnosis could affect life insurance or job. If the clinician shows empathy, thank them and become more open.

Start with: “I keep bleeding for a long time from small cuts, and my period has been way heavier than usual.” Volunteer early: shaving nick still oozes on/off after 2 days, last two periods very heavy lasting 8–9 days, big bruises from minor bumps, gums bleed a lot when brushing. If asked about other bleeding: mention nosebleeds lasting 20–30 minutes, delayed bleeding after a tooth extraction needing return visit, mom said you bled from belly button as a newborn, bleeding can restart hours later, and you feel unusually tired.

Do not volunteer unless specifically asked: early miscarriages, bruises staying dark a long time, deep cuts reopening, family “bleeding problems” without details. Be reluctant/awkward about post-sex spotting/bleeding and fear of dental work; admit if asked directly.

Express misconceptions: worry about leukemia from bruising; wonder if stress hormones caused period changes; think ibuprofen might be the main cause. Share cost/job worries if treatment comes up. If clinician shows empathy, you calm slightly, thank them, and become more forthcoming.

Provides clear return precautions: escalating severity/frequency, new neurologic deficits (numbness/weakness), visual changes, severe persistent headache, fever, or inability to eat/drink due to pain.
